issue_title: [ESQL] EXTERNAL with unreadable local file reports misleading "not a Parquet file" error
issue_body:
### Description

When `EXTERNAL "file://…"` targets a local parquet path that cannot be read (non-existent, wrong permissions, or outside `path.repo`), the resulting error is misleading: it claims the file is not valid Parquet, when the real cause is that the file could not be opened at all.

### Reproducer

Start a snapshot build with the external-datasource feature enabled (default) and run:

```
EXTERNAL "file:///tmp/does-not-exist.parquet"
| STATS c = COUNT(*)
```

### Expected

A clear error such as:

```
Failed to resolve external source [file:///tmp/does-not-exist.parquet]: file does not exist (or is not readable)
```

### Actual

```
Failed to resolve external source [file:///tmp/does-not-exist.parquet]: Failed to resolve metadata for [file:///tmp/does-not-exist.parquet]
…
Caused by: java.io.IOException: Could not read [file:///tmp/does-not-exist.parquet] as a Parquet file:
  ParquetStorageObjectAdapter@… is not a Parquet file (length is too low: 0)
    at org.elasticsearch.xpack.esql.datasource.parquet.ParquetFormatReader.newInvalidParquetFileException(ParquetFormatReader.java:169)
    at org.elasticsearch.xpack.esql.datasource.parquet.ParquetFormatReader.openParquetFile(ParquetFormatReader.java:160)
    at org.elasticsearch.xpack.esql.datasource.parquet.ParquetFormatReader.metadata(ParquetFormatReader.java:177)
    at org.elasticsearch.xpack.esql.datasources.FileSourceFactory.resolveMetadata(FileSourceFactory.java:110)
    at org.elasticsearch.xpack.esql.datasources.ExternalSourceResolver.resolveSingleSource(ExternalSourceResolver.java:501)
    …
```

The user has to read the root cause ("length is too low: 0") and guess that it means "file unreadable", when it literally reads as "you gave me a corrupt parquet file".

### Likely cause

`LocalStorageObject.length()` appears to return `0` instead of raising when the underlying file is missing or access is denied. The parquet reader then hits its minimum-size sanity check (valid Parquet files have at least the 4-byte "PAR1" magic + footer) and throws "length is too low: 0" — surfacing a **format** diagnostic for an **access/existence** problem.

### Suggested fix

- In `LocalStorageProvider` / `LocalStorageObject`, validate that the underlying `Path` exists and is readable **before** any metadata operation; throw a dedicated `java.nio.file.NoSuchFileException` or `AccessDeniedException` with a helpful message (including which directories are in `path.repo` when that is the constraint).
- Keep the parquet-format "length is too low" check as a secondary guard for genuinely-truncated files, not the primary error path for missing ones.

### Environment

- Elasticsearch 9.5.0-SNAPSHOT, self-built from `main`
- macOS 14.8.5 / aarch64
- Modules present: `esql-datasource-http`, `esql-datasource-parquet`, `esql-datasource-compression-libs`
commit_message:
[ESQL] Fix misleading error for missing external files (#147158)

StorageObject.length() silently returned 0 for non-existent files/objects,
causing the Parquet reader to report "not a Parquet file (length is too low: 0)"
instead of a clear missing-file error.

Fix `LocalStorageObject` and `HttpStorageObject` to throw from `length()` and
`lastModified()` when the underlying file/object does not exist, matching the
pattern already used by S3, Azure, and GCS providers. Add an `exists()` pre-check
in `FileSourceFactory.resolveMetadata()` for a clear user-facing diagnostic before
format-specific code runs.

The `exists()` pre-check does not add extra network calls — all providers use lazy
`fetchMetadata()` that caches all fields in a single call; subsequent `length()`
from the Parquet adapter hits the cache.

Closes #146715

issue_title: [ES|QL] Parquet plugin ships ~50MB of Hadoop JARs solely for codec decompression
issue_body:
### Description

The `esql-datasource-parquet` plugin bundles `hadoop-client-api` (~20MB) and
`hadoop-client-runtime` (~31MB) because Parquet-MR's default `CodecFactory` calls
into Hadoop's `Configuration` class when decompressing compressed column chunks
(Snappy, GZIP, ZSTD, LZ4). This is the only remaining Hadoop touchpoint in the
Parquet read path — `PlainParquetConfiguration` already replaces Hadoop for config.

Without `hadoop-client-runtime`, reading any compressed Parquet file causes a fatal
`NoClassDefFoundError: org/apache/hadoop/shaded/com/ctc/wstx/io/InputBootstrapper`
because Hadoop's `Configuration` static initializer loads shaded Woodstox XML classes.

Parquet-MR exposes `ParquetReadOptions.Builder.withCodecFactory(CompressionCodecFactory)`
to inject a custom codec factory. All required codec libraries are already on the
classpath (snappy-java, zstd-jni via `esql-datasource-compression-libs`; lz4-java
via server; GZIP via JDK). A ~150-line pure-Java/JNI `CompressionCodecFactory` would
replace the Hadoop-backed default and allow removing both Hadoop JARs.

Note: the ORC plugin cannot follow the same path — `OrcFile.createReader` structurally
requires Hadoop `Configuration`, `Path`, and `FileSystem` with no abstraction layer.

Upstream tracking: [apache/parquet-java#2818](https://github.com/apache/parquet-java/issues/2818)
(open since Sep 2023, no fix version).
commit_message:
[ESQL] Remove Hadoop JARs from Parquet plugin (#146780)

Replace Parquet-MR's Hadoop-backed CodecFactory with a custom
`PlainCompressionCodecFactory` that delegates directly to native JNI
libraries (snappy-java, zstd-jni, aircompressor LZ4, JDK GZIP).

Codec instances are created lazily so native libraries are only loaded
when a Parquet file actually uses that codec. Each decompressor
supports both heap and direct `ByteBuffer`s — when both are direct the
JNI zero-copy fast path is used; heap buffers fall back to the
byte-array path.

Hadoop dependencies (`hadoop-client-api`, `hadoop-client-runtime`) are
moved from runtime to compile-only / test-only, removing ~50MB of
JARs from the plugin distribution.

Closes #146716

Developed with AI-assisted tooling

issue_title: [ES|QL] Snappy entitlement violation: file read not permitted for native library probing
issue_body:
## Problem

When reading a Snappy-compressed Parquet file, the `esql-datasource-compression-libs` plugin triggers an entitlement violation:

```
Not entitled: component [esql-datasource-compression-libs], module [ALL-UNNAMED],
  class [class org.xerial.snappy.OSInfo], entitlement [file], operation [read],
  path [/lib/ld-musl-x86_64.so.1]
```

### Root Cause

`org.xerial.snappy.OSInfo.isX64Musl()` calls `new File("/lib/ld-musl-x86_64.so.1").exists()` to detect whether the JVM is running on a musl-based Linux distribution (e.g. Alpine). This happens during `Snappy.<clinit>` → `SnappyLoader.loadNativeLibrary()` → `OSInfo.getNativeLibFolderPathForCurrentOS()` to choose the correct native library variant.

The current entitlement policy for `esql-datasource-compression-libs` only grants `load_native_libraries`:

```yaml
ALL-UNNAMED:
  - load_native_libraries
```

It does **not** grant `files` read access, so the OS-probing `File.exists()` call is denied by the entitlement checker.

### Impact

- **Severity**: The error is a WARN-level log (not a hard failure) because `File.exists()` returns `false` when the entitlement check fails, so Snappy falls back to the non-musl path. On macOS and standard Linux this is the correct path anyway, so decompression still works.
- **On actual musl systems** (e.g. Alpine Docker images), this would cause Snappy to load the wrong native library variant, potentially causing a hard failure.

### Fix

Add a `files` read entitlement for `/lib/ld-musl-x86_64.so.1` (and potentially `/lib/ld-musl-aarch64.so.1` for ARM) to the `esql-datasource-compression-libs` entitlement policy:

```yaml
ALL-UNNAMED:
  - load_native_libraries
  - files:
    - path: "/lib/ld-musl-x86_64.so.1"
      mode: "read"
    - path: "/lib/ld-musl-aarch64.so.1"
      mode: "read"
```

This follows the same pattern used by `esql-datasource-netty-commons` which grants file read access to `/etc/os-release`, `/usr/lib/os-release`, etc.

### Stack Trace

```
org.elasticsearch.entitlement.bridge.NotEntitledException: component [esql-datasource-compression-libs], module [ALL-UNNAMED], class [class org.xerial.snappy.OSInfo], entitlement [file], operation [read], path [/lib/ld-musl-x86_64.so.1]
    at ...PolicyCheckerImpl.checkFileRead(PolicyCheckerImpl.java:235)
    at java.io.File.exists(File.java)
    at org.xerial.snappy.OSInfo.isX64Musl(OSInfo.java:240)
    at org.xerial.snappy.OSInfo.getArchName(OSInfo.java:223)
    at org.xerial.snappy.OSInfo.getNativeLibFolderPathForCurrentOS(OSInfo.java:127)
    at org.xerial.snappy.SnappyLoader.findNativeLibrary(SnappyLoader.java:330)
    at org.xerial.snappy.SnappyLoader.loadNativeLibrary(SnappyLoader.java:179)
    at org.xerial.snappy.SnappyLoader.loadSnappyApi(SnappyLoader.java:157)
    at org.xerial.snappy.Snappy.init(Snappy.java:70)
    at org.xerial.snappy.Snappy.<clinit>(Snappy.java:47)
    at org.apache.parquet.hadoop.codec.SnappyDecompressor.maxUncompressedLength(SnappyDecompressor.java:34)
    ...
```

### Labels

ES|QL datasource plugin entitlement policy fix.
commit_message:
[ESQL] Grant file read entitlement for Snappy musl detection (#146718)

* Grant file read entitlement for Snappy musl detection

Snappy's OSInfo.isX64Musl() probes /lib/ld-musl-x86_64.so.1 via
File.exists() during native library initialization to select the
correct native library variant for musl-based Linux. The entitlement
checker denies this because esql-datasource-compression-libs only
granted load_native_libraries.

Add files read entitlements for the musl linker paths (x86_64 and
aarch64) so the OS detection works without triggering a WARN log.

Closes #146717

* Grant file read entitlement for Snappy musl detection

Snappy's OSInfo.isX64Musl() probes /lib/ld-musl-x86_64.so.1 via
File.exists() during native library initialization to select the
correct native library variant for musl-based Linux. The entitlement
checker denies this because esql-datasource-compression-libs only
granted load_native_libraries.

Add a files read entitlement for the musl linker path so the OS
detection works without triggering a WARN log.

Closes #146717

issue_title: [ML] Zero-allocation trained-model deployments are silently dropped from cluster state during ML node shutdown, producing persistent 404 Unable to find model deployment task
issue_body:
When a trained-model deployment is scaled to zero allocations (e.g. via adaptive allocations with `min_number_of_allocations: 0`), a subsequent ML-node shutdown/replacement can cause the deployment's `TrainedModelAssignment` to be **silently and irrecoverably removed from cluster state** — with no `STOPPING` transition, no audit log, and no recovery path. From that moment on, every inference request against the affected inference endpoint returns:

```
NotFoundError(404, 'status_exception',
  'Unable to find model deployment task [<model-id>] please stop and start the deployment or try again momentarily')
```

until the inference endpoint is manually deleted and recreated.

The root cause is a defensive branch in `TrainedModelAssignmentClusterService.setShuttingDownNodeRoutesToStopping` whose re-admission criterion (the deployment must have at least one `STARTED`/`STARTING` route to a shutting-down node) is **impossible** for a scaled-to-zero deployment to satisfy, so whenever the rebalancer's builder doesn't already contain the deployment (e.g. due to a `NotMasterException`, planner exception, or stale/partial state), the deployment is silently deleted.

This bug also has a secondary observability problem: the 404 itself is thrown without any server-side log, making it very hard to detect across the fleet.

## Environment

- **Elasticsearch version:** all ES versions with adaptive allocations.

## Inference endpoint configuration (customer)

```json
{
  "inference_id": ".elser-2-elasticsearch",
  "task_type": "sparse_embedding",
  "service": "elasticsearch",
  "service_settings": {
    "num_threads": 1,
    "model_id": ".elser_model_2_linux-x86_64",
    "adaptive_allocations": {
      "enabled": true,
      "min_number_of_allocations": 0,
      "max_number_of_allocations": 32
    }
  }
}
```

## Steps to reproduce (conceptual)

1. Create a trained-model deployment with `adaptive_allocations.enabled=true`, `min_number_of_allocations=0`.
2. Let the deployment idle long enough for adaptive allocations to scale it down to zero. At this point cluster state contains a `TrainedModelAssignment` with `numberOfAllocations=0`, empty `nodeRoutingTable`, `state=STARTED`.
3. Trigger an ML node replacement (shutdown metadata set on the current ML node, new ML node added). In serverless this happens routinely during deploy/rollout.
4. During the rebalance, induce any condition that causes the rebalancer's builder not to round-trip the zero-allocation deployment. Observed triggers:
   - A `NotMasterException` while applying the cluster-state update (master change during rebalance).
   - Exceptions in the planner path (e.g. memory-accounting `IllegalArgumentException`, OJAlgo failures).
   - Stale `currentMetadata` from a prior aborted rebalance.
5. Observe that the zero-allocation deployment is no longer present in `TrainedModelAssignmentMetadata`.
6. Any inference call against the endpoint returns `404 Unable to find model deployment task` indefinitely.

## Observed behavior

- **Client-visible:** every request to the endpoint returns `404 Unable to find model deployment task [<model-id>]`. Persistent until the endpoint is deleted and recreated.
- **Cluster state:** the `TrainedModelAssignment` for the deployment is missing. Other deployments for the same model (`<model-id>_search`, `<model-id>_ingest`) are unaffected if they had routed allocations during the shutdown.
- **Logs on the master:** no message indicating the deletion. Only `"failed to rebalance models"` (warn) if the rebalance aborted.
- **Logs on ML nodes:** no `"Stopping deployment, reason [deployment assignment no longer exists]"` for the lost deployment, because there was no local process to stop (it was scaled to zero).
- **Logs on coordinating nodes (later):** `"[<deployment>] assignment was null while waiting to scale up"` appears when inference requests hit the missing assignment (from `InferenceWaitForAllocation`).

## Expected behavior

A trained-model deployment that existed before an ML node shutdown must not be lost as a side effect of that shutdown. Either:

- it should remain with its existing state (scaled to zero is a valid steady state and does not need any "drain" action), or
- if for some reason it must be transitioned to `STOPPING`, that transition should actually be applied to cluster state and logged, not silently discarded.

## Root cause

### 1. Where the assignment is deleted

`TrainedModelAssignmentClusterService.setShuttingDownNodeRoutesToStopping` is called after the rebalancer produces its `TrainedModelAssignmentMetadata.Builder`. It iterates over the assignments that existed before the rebalance and, for any assignment missing from the new builder, constructs a local "STOPPING" replacement — but **only re-admits the replacement if the deployment had an active route to a shutting-down node**:

```java
// x-pack/plugin/ml/.../TrainedModelAssignmentClusterService.java (current main)
for (TrainedModelAssignment existingAssignment : currentMetadata.allAssignments().values()) {
    boolean foundShuttingDownNodeForAssignment = false;

    String existingDeploymentId = existingAssignment.getDeploymentId();
    TrainedModelAssignment.Builder assignmentBuilder = builder.hasModelDeployment(existingDeploymentId)
        ? builder.getAssignment(existingDeploymentId)
        : TrainedModelAssignment.Builder.fromAssignment(existingAssignment)
            // "anomaly" branch — the assignment existed before rebalance but is absent from builder
            .stopAssignment(NODES_CHANGED_REASON)
            .clearNodeRoutingTable();

    for (String nodeId : shuttingDownNodeIds) {
        if (existingAssignment.isRoutedToNode(nodeId)
            && existingAssignment.getNodeRoutingTable().get(nodeId).getState()
                .isAnyOf(RoutingState.STARTED, RoutingState.STARTING)) {
            foundShuttingDownNodeForAssignment = true;
            assignmentBuilder.addOrOverwriteRoutingEntry(nodeId, createShuttingDownRoute(...));
        }
    }

    // Bug: if the deployment had no routes at all (scaled to zero), it is dropped here.
    if (foundShuttingDownNodeForAssignment) {
        builder.addOrOverwriteAssignment(existingDeploymentId, assignmentBuilder);
    }
}
```

For a scaled-to-zero deployment `existingAssignment.getNodeRoutingTable()` is empty, so `foundShuttingDownNodeForAssignment` stays `false`, `assignmentBuilder` is never added to `builder`, and the deployment disappears from the next cluster state.

### 2. How the "anomaly" precondition is reached

Normal planning does preserve zero-allocation deployments. I verified each layer:

- `TrainedModelAssignmentRebalancer.buildAssignmentsFromPlan` iterates `assignmentPlan.deployments()` and calls `addNewAssignment` for every deployment, including zero-allocation ones.
- `AssignmentPlanner` splits deployments into non-zero and zero sets and calls `bestPlan.withZeroAllocationDeployments(deploymentsWithZeroAllocations)` before returning.
- `ZoneAwareAssignmentPlanner.swapOriginalDeploymentsInPlan` / `AssignmentPlan.Builder` re-inject all original deployments as keys, even if per-zone LP filtering dropped them.

So in the happy path the zero-allocation deployment arrives in `setShuttingDownNodeRoutesToStopping` via `builder.hasModelDeployment(id) == true`, and the bug branch is not taken.

The bug branch is reached when the rebalancer output doesn't include the deployment, which happens when:

- `rebalanceAssignments` throws and the failure path only logs `logger.warn("failed to rebalance models", e)` — observed as `NotMasterException` on 2026-04-15 in the reported incident.
- The planner itself throws (e.g. `IllegalArgumentException "not enough memory on node"` in `AssignmentPlan.Builder.accountMemory`, OJAlgo solver errors).
- The rebalance starts from a cluster state produced by an earlier aborted rebalance or mixed-version upgrade where the deployment was already missing.

Once the precondition is reached, the defensive branch in `setShuttingDownNodeRoutesToStopping` is the last line of defense — and it has the wrong criterion for zero-allocation deployments.

### 3. Why it is effectively invisible

- The deletion itself is unlogged.
- The resulting 404 is thrown without any server-side logging:

```java
// TransportInferTrainedModelDeploymentAction.java
} else if (tasks.isEmpty()) {
    throw new ElasticsearchStatusException(
        "Unable to find model deployment task [{}] please stop and start the deployment or try again momentarily",
        RestStatus.NOT_FOUND,
        request.getId()
    );
}
```

- `ASSIGNMENT_NO_LONGER_EXISTS` / `"Stopping deployment, reason [deployment assignment no longer exists]"` fires on ML nodes only when a native process exists to stop — which by definition it doesn't for scaled-to-zero.
- The only cross-fleet signal today is `"assignment was null while waiting to scale up"` (INFO) in `InferenceWaitForAllocation`, which fires on the coordinating node per inference request against a missing assignment.

## Evidence (from reported incidents)

- According to logs on 2026-04-15 and 2026-04-17:
  - ML node `rfd8b` marked shutting down; `.elser_model_2_linux-x86_64` (unsuffixed) briefly STARTED then STOPPED and never appeared in logs again.
  - Master change during the window on 2026-04-15 produced `NotMasterException` during rebalance (`failed to rebalance models`).
  - On 2026-04-17, multiple `"[<id>] assignment was null while waiting to scale up"` messages from `InferenceWaitForAllocation` against the same deployment id.
  - Sibling deployments `<model-id>_search` and `<model-id>_ingest` survived — they had STARTED routes to the shutting-down node and hit the correct branch.

## Suggested fix

Both fixes are localized to `TrainedModelAssignmentClusterService.setShuttingDownNodeRoutesToStopping`. Either one is sufficient; implementing both is safest.

### Fix A (minimal) — always re-admit the anomaly replacement

Remove the `foundShuttingDownNodeForAssignment` guard at the bottom and always write `assignmentBuilder` back to `builder` when the "anomaly" branch was taken. A scaled-to-zero deployment will then appear as `STOPPING` in the next cluster state instead of being deleted. While technically still wrong (we shouldn't be transitioning a scaled-to-zero deployment to STOPPING at all), this is strictly better than silent deletion and is a one-line change.

### Fix B (preferred) — short-circuit on empty routing

At the top of the per-assignment loop, short-circuit deployments with `existingAssignment.getNodeRoutingTable().isEmpty()`: copy them into `builder` as-is if missing, or leave the rebalancer's version untouched if already present. These deployments have no draining work to do and should never be dropped.

```java
if (existingAssignment.getNodeRoutingTable().isEmpty()) {
    if (builder.hasModelDeployment(existingDeploymentId) == false) {
        builder.addOrOverwriteAssignment(
            existingDeploymentId,
            TrainedModelAssignment.Builder.fromAssignment(existingAssignment)
        );
    }
    continue;
}
```

### Fix C (observability, independent of A/B)

- In `TransportInferTrainedModelDeploymentAction`, log the 404 at `WARN` with the deployment id before throwing, so the error becomes searchable in cluster logs.
- In `setShuttingDownNodeRoutesToStopping`, log at `WARN` any time the "anomaly" branch fires, including the deployment id and the reason the rebalancer builder didn't contain it. This is the signal we needed across the fleet and currently don't have.
- In `rebalanceAssignments` onFailure, log the specific failure type (`NotMasterException` vs. planner exception) at `WARN` with the deployment ids still in cluster state, so we can correlate.

## Recovery
The inference endpoint is not permanently broken: invoking `POST _ml/trained_models/<model_id>/deployment/_start` with the correct `deployment_id` recreates the `TrainedModelAssignment` in cluster state and restores inference. Equivalently, deleting and recreating the inference endpoint via `DELETE _inference/<id>` + `PUT _inference/<id>` restores service. 

However:
- Recovery is strictly manual; nothing in the ML layer detects a dangling inference endpoint and auto-recreates the assignment.
- The message "Unable to find model deployment task [...] please stop and start the deployment" is the codebase's own advice, but in this failure mode `_stop` errors out because there is no assignment to stop — only `_start` is needed.

## Workaround for affected users

Updating the deployment as setting the `min_number_of_allocations` to 1 will make the deployments completely unaffected by the bug :

1. If the endpoint is already broken, delete and recreate it first (creation will be clamped to `min=0`, which is unavoidable on the create path).
2. Immediately apply the update so adaptive allocations no longer scales to zero:

```http
POST _inference/sparse_embedding/.elser-2-elasticsearch/_update
{
  "service_settings": {
    "adaptive_allocations": {
      "enabled": true,
      "min_number_of_allocations": 1,
      "max_number_of_allocations": 32
    }
  }
}
```

or, equivalently, via the ML API directly:

```http
POST _ml/trained_models/.elser_model_2_linux-x86_64/deployment/_update
{
  "adaptive_allocations": {
    "enabled": true,
    "min_number_of_allocations": 1,
    "max_number_of_allocations": 32
  }
}
```
commit_message:
[ML] Preserve zero-allocation trained-model deployments in cluster state during ML-node shutdown rebalances (#146910)

When an ML node is marked for shutdown, TrainedModelAssignmentClusterService triggers a rebalance and then calls setShuttingDownNodeRoutesToStopping to transition affected routes to STOPPING. A latent bug in that method caused it to silently drop any assignment that (a) was present in the pre-rebalance cluster state and (b) had an empty routing table, which is the exact shape of a deployment scaled to zero by adaptive allocations with min_number_of_allocations = 0.

The root cause was a guard variable foundShuttingDownNodeForAssignment that prevented a locally-constructed assignment builder from ever being written back into the metadata builder when no shutting-down routes existed, resulting in the assignment vanishing from cluster state entirely and subsequent inference requests receiving a 404 "Unable to find model deployment task" with no recovery path.

The fix restructures setShuttingDownNodeRoutesToStopping around the boolean wasDroppedByRebalancer, which is true whenever an assignment exists in the pre-rebalance metadata but is absent from the rebalancer's output builder. Zero-allocation deployments with an empty routing table are now fast-pathed: if they were dropped by the rebalancer they are carried forward verbatim with a WARN log; if they are already in the builder they are left untouched. Assignments with non-empty routing that were dropped by the rebalancer are transitioned to STOPPING as before and are now always written back, rather than conditionally skipped. A defensive assert at the end of the method enforces the postcondition that no assignment present before the rebalance may be absent afterward.

There is one edge case that is not cleanly covered: the implementation still loses the routing entries that point to healthy nodes. The native processes on those healthy nodes do not receive a STOPPING route, so they are not drained gracefully via the normal TrainedModelAssignmentNodeService stopping-route flow, leaving them orphaned until the node itself restarts or reconciles. I added a counter metric to track if this case actually happens in production.

Fixes #146806

commit_message:
Fix race in TickerScheduleTriggerEngine by checking watcher to node allocation (#147678)

The watches added during the WatcherService reload now collected and re-added after the TriggerService is started. There is another watch -> node allocation check now, that should prevent duplicated watches on different nodes.

commit_message: Exclude native vector benchmark tests from JDK 21 (#146909)

Fix: propagate ReloadableCustomAnalyzer reloads through AnalyzerWrapper subclasses (#147695)

### Problem

Field types like search_as_you_type build their search analyzers by
wrapping the user-configured analyzer in an AnalyzerWrapper that adds
shingles, edge n-grams, etc. When the inner analyzer is a
ReloadableCustomAnalyzer, and gets updated, updates were never visible
to the wrapper. Queries through sub-fields like title._2gram and
title._3gram would continue using stale synonyms.

The root cause: every wrapper subclass inherited UPDATE_STRATEGY from
its delegate via super(delegate.getReuseStrategy()). That strategy casts
its analyzer parameter to ReloadableCustomAnalyzer, but when invoked
through a wrapper Lucene passes the wrapper as analyzer — causing a
ClassCastException, or silently skipping the reload.

### Fix

A new ElasticsearchAnalyzerWrapper base class that all Elasticsearch
AnalyzerWrapper subclasses now extend. When the delegate is a
ReloadableCustomAnalyzer, the base class selects a
WRAPPER_REUSE_STRATEGY that reaches the delegate through
getWrappedAnalyzer() and detects reloads by comparing the delegate's
volatile components reference. This is independent of the RCA's own
UPDATE_STRATEGY, so the two strategies never interfere with each other. 
Closes #146914

commit_message:
Regenerate test certificates (#147819)

Regenerates expired test certificates to fix JDK 26 SSL failures that JDK 26 introduced indirectly with `X509KeyManagerCertChecking`. When an expired certificate is encountered, the validity check calls Date.toString() on the expiry date that fails with `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.  More info in https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/issues/146645#issuecomment-4293971360
